natural_occurrence.description: Calcium chloride occurs naturally in brine and sea water, but is commercially produced as a byproduct of the Solvay process or by treating limestone with hydrochloric acid.

manufacturing.description: Produced as a byproduct of the Solvay process (sodium carbonate production) or by reacting calcium carbonate (limestone) or calcium hydroxide with hydrochloric acid. Purified through crystallization and drying.

other_uses.cosmetics: Viscosity regulator, stabilizer in personal care products.
other_uses.industrial: De-icing roads and sidewalks, concrete acceleration, desiccant, dust control on unpaved roads.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Electrolyte replenisher, calcium supplement, treatment for hypocalcemia.
other_uses.household: DampRid moisture absorber, aquarium water hardener.

eu.conditions: Permitted in various food categories as firming agent and stabilizer.

usa.notes: Widely used in food processing and molecular gastronomy.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

food_categories_eu.category_name: Canned or bottled fruit and vegetables

fun_facts: THE secret ingredient in MOLECULAR GASTRONOMY for 'reverse spherification' - creates those liquid-filled spheres that pop in your mouth
Used by modernist chefs like Ferran Adrià at elBulli to create iconic dishes like 'olive oil spheres'
Keeps pickles crunchy and prevents canned vegetables from turning to mush
The same chemical used to de-ice roads in winter (food grade is much purer)
Generates heat when dissolved in water - it's an exothermic reaction you can feel
Used in tofu production in Asia for centuries as a traditional coagulant
Can make a slightly salty/bitter taste if used in large amounts, so used sparingly in food
